All numeric features are standardized (z-score) using training-set statistics. All preprocessing transformers—scaler, encoder, imputer—are fit on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This is critical for avoiding data leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The hybrid model is a CNN-RNN architecture: a convolutional neural network processes temporal sequences of treatments (procedures, medications) as feature extractors, feeding into an LSTM recurrent layer to capture temporal dependencies. Dense layers integrate CNN/RNN outputs with static features, culminating in a sigmoid output for binary prediction. Architecture: 2 CNN blocks (32 and 64 filters, kernel size 3), 1 LSTM layer (64 units), 2 dense layers (128 and 32 units with ReLU), dropout (0.3), learning rate 0.001.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on identical features (excluding raw sequences, which logistic regression cannot process natively, but including all engineered features). Logistic regression is tuned via grid search over L2 regularization strength (C ∈ {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10}) on the validation set. This ensures the baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter optimization effort and fair feature engineering, making any performance gap attributable to the hybrid model's architecture rather than tuning disparity.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Readmission data are imbalanced (approximately 15–20% positive rate). AUPRC is insensitive to class imbalance and directly reflects the clinically relevant trade-off between precision (avoiding false alarms that waste intervention resources) and recall (identifying true high-risk patients). This aligns with hospital administrators' operational goal: resource-constrained intervention programs need both sensitivity and specificity.

Secondary metrics include: (1) sensitivity at 90% specificity, reflecting the clinical constraint that false positives are costly; (2) F1-score (macro-averaged) for balanced precision-recall assessment; (3) calibration diagnostics (Hosmer-Lemeshow test and calibration plots) to ensure predicted probabilities reflect true readmission risk—essential for clinicians interpreting model outputs; (4) feature attribution via SHAP values (hybrid model) and permutation importance (logistic regression) to address interpretability requirements.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning for both models occurs on the validation set only. The model configuration achieving the best AUPRC on the validation set is selected. The test set is held out and accessed only once, after tuning is complete. No cross-validation is performed on the full dataset because temporal structure would be violated by random k-fold splits; temporal split is methodologically appropriate here.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Department variability is addressed via stratified splitting and feature inclusion. Department-level baseline readmission rates (computed from training data) are incorporated as features, enabling both models to learn department-specific risk adjustments. Sensitivity analysis evaluates performance within each department on the test set to verify the hybrid model does not improve performance in some departments while degrading in others. Small departments (n < 500) are flagged as analytical limitations.

The experiment establishes whether the hybrid model achieves higher AUPRC than logistic regression on held-out test data. It does not establish whether the hybrid model is clinically deployable without human validation, whether the performance gains persist across hospitals with different EHR systems, or whether the model remains fair across demographic subgroups—those are separate validation steps.

hypothesis: Using ensemble methods that combine historical patient treatment data with real-time clinical notes leads to more accurate predictions of patient readmission risk compared to a single model using only historical treatment data.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for readmission prediction
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Ensemble Methods for Readmission Risk Prediction

### Objective
To test whether an ensemble combining gradient-boosted models on historical treatment data with logistic regression on clinical notes outperforms a baseline logistic regression model trained on historical data alone, in predicting 30-day patient readmission risk.

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy
The experiment uses electronic health records from a major hospital system spanning 5 years, encompassing 50,000+ patient admissions. This data represents the deployment target and contains all required information: structured EHR fields (demographics, diagnoses, medications, lab values, admission timing), unstructured clinical notes (admission, progress, and discharge notes), and outcome data (readmission events).

The target variable is defined prospectively: readmission within 30 days of discharge. Any acute care admission occurring ≥24 hours and ≤30 days after discharge is marked as readmission; planned readmissions (elective procedures identified via admission codes) are excluded. This definition aligns with hospital quality metrics and CMS reporting standards, ensuring the model targets clinically and operationally relevant outcomes.

### 2. Split Strategy and Leakage Prevention
Data is split temporally by discharge date: training (first 60%, ~30,000 admissions), validation (next 20%, ~10,000 admissions), and test (final 20%, ~10,000 admissions). Within each temporal segment, stratification by readmission class preserves label distribution.

Temporal splitting is essential because patient outcomes and clinical trajectories develop over time. A random split would allow a model trained on later data to see patterns from earlier clinical notes or treatment changes that occurred after the index admission, creating information leakage. Temporal ordering ensures that the training set contains only information available at or before discharge, and the validation and test sets represent true future predictions.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Structured features include: age, sex, race/ethnicity, admission type, primary diagnosis (ICD code), comorbidity burden, prior readmission history, length of stay, discharge medication count, and laboratory values recorded at admission or during the stay. Temporal features (day of week, season) capture time-dependent patterns.

Clinical notes are preprocessed via lowercasing, removal of protected health information patterns, tokenization, and TF-IDF vectorization (top 500 features). All preprocessing transformers are fit exclusively on the training set, then applied to validation and test sets. This ensures test-set statistics do not influence model development. No feature is derived from the readmission outcome or post-discharge information.

### 4. Model and Baseline
The proposed ensemble combines two models:
- **Model A (Structured):** XGBoost trained on structured features only.
- **Model B (Clinical Notes):** Logistic regression trained on TF-IDF vectors from clinical notes.
- **Ensemble:** Weighted average of Model A and Model B predictions (weights tuned on validation set).

The baseline is a logistic regression model trained on structured features only, representing current practice (historical data without clinical notes). Both the ensemble and baseline receive identical hyperparameter tuning effort via grid search on the validation set, using the primary metric (AUROC). This ensures the comparison is fair and isolates the contribution of clinical notes and ensemble methodology.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation
The validation set is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning. Grid search over hyperparameter configurations (learning rate, regularization, ensemble weights) is performed, and the configuration with the highest AUROC on the validation set is selected. The test set is held out and evaluated exactly once, after all model decisions are finalized, to produce the definitive performance report.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric: AUROC.** Readmission prediction is a decision-support task where clinicians apply thresholds to identify high-risk patients for intervention. AUROC measures discriminative ability across all thresholds, is robust to class imbalance, and directly aligns with stakeholder objectives: better discrimination enables more effective identification and prevention of readmissions.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision-Recall AUC (performance in the positive class), calibration error (probability reliability for clinical decisions), sensitivity at high specificity (operating-point performance), and PPV at F1-optimized thresholds (practical deployment effectiveness).

### 7. Controls for Confounds
Demographic stratification: Performance is evaluated separately by age, sex, and race/ethnicity to verify that ensemble improvements are consistent across demographic groups and not driven by demographic confounding. Health system variation: Rolling analysis across hospital sites and time windows checks for robustness. Baseline fairness: Equal tuning effort for baseline and ensemble prevents confounding by differential model optimization. These controls establish whether the ensemble advantage is genuine and generalizable or artifacts of specific populations or time periods.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The temporal split may reduce training data diversity and should be replaced with a random split to improve model generalization.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is required to prevent information leakage in clinical forecasting; using a random split would allow future data to influence training, violating the real-world deployment assumption where predictions must be made without knowledge of future patient trajectories.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The baseline model uses logistic regression while the ensemble includes XGBoost, making the comparison unfair due to differences in model capacity.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The hypothesis specifically tests the value of combining clinical notes with structured data via ensemble methods; the baseline appropriately mirrors common practice (logistic regression on structured data) and both models undergo equal hyperparameter tuning, ensuring a fair comparison of architectural and data contributions.

must_not_claim_details.claim: AUROC is not clinically meaningful and should be replaced with a threshold-based metric like F1-score or precision at a fixed recall level.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: AUROC is the appropriate primary metric because it evaluates discriminative performance across all possible thresholds, which aligns with stakeholder needs in a decision-support context where optimal thresholds may vary by patient population or clinical setting; threshold-specific metrics are included as secondary evaluations.

hypothesis: A convolutional neural network (CNN) designed for analyzing echocardiogram images predicts heart disease risk more accurately than traditional clinical assessments, using a cohort of diverse patients over 5 years.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML - Cardiology
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: CNN-Based Echocardiogram Risk Prediction vs. Traditional Clinical Assessment

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We would leverage 5 years of echocardiogram imaging data (Jan 2019–Dec 2023) from a multi-site hospital network, covering approximately 10,000 patients. For each patient, we would obtain:
- **Imaging data:** Standardized 2D echocardiogram sequences (typically 256×256 pixels, 20–30 frames per acquisition, grayscale).
- **Clinical outcomes:** Continuous heart disease risk scores (0–100) derived from standardized measures including ejection fraction, left ventricular mass index, diastolic dysfunction grade, and validated prognostic indices (e.g., MAGGIC score components). These scores are assigned at baseline (within 30 days of imaging) and at 12-month follow-up, with follow-up scores serving as the ground truth label.
- **Tabular features:** Patient demographics (age, sex, BMI), prior clinical assessments (blood pressure, cholesterol panel, diabetes and smoking status), site identifier, and imaging equipment model.

All outcomes are adjudicated by experienced cardiologists blinded to model predictions, and extracted from structured EHR systems to minimize transcription error. Imaging protocols are documented by site and equipment model; any deviations from standard acquisition are flagged in preprocessing.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

We employ a **stratified temporal split with site-based stratification** to address both temporal dependencies and imaging protocol heterogeneity.
- **Temporal ordering:** Train (60%, Jan 2019–Oct 2021, n≈6,000) → Validation (20%, Nov 2021–Jun 2022, n≈2,000) → Test (20%, Jul 2022–Dec 2023, n≈2,000). This chronological ordering prevents future clinical outcomes from leaking into the training window and reflects the deployment scenario where models make predictions on prospective patient data.
- **Stratification axes:** Within each time window, patients are stratified by (a) baseline risk quintile (to preserve outcome distribution and catch potential class imbalance across severity levels), (b) imaging site (to ensure balanced representation of each hospital's imaging protocol), and (c) random within-stratum shuffling to prevent ordering bias.
- **Justification:** Temporal splits respect the sequential nature of clinical data and deployment reality. Site stratification ensures that train, validation, and test sets are representative of the full hospital network, preventing a model trained on one site's equipment from being evaluated on another's without warning.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers are **fit on the training set only** and applied identically to validation and test sets:
- **Image normalization:** Grayscale intensity is normalized to zero mean and unit variance using statistics computed from the training set. Image augmentation (rotation ±15°, brightness ±10%, elastic deformation) is applied only during training to improve robustness; validation and test images are processed without augmentation.
- **Tabular features:** Missing values in clinical assessments are imputed using median values (or k-nearest neighbors) computed from the training set. Categorical features (site, equipment model, smoking/diabetes status) are one-hot encoded using the training set's vocabulary; any unseen categories in val/test are mapped to a reserved "other" category.
- **No data leakage:** No derived features (e.g., interaction terms or risk ratios) that could encode the label are constructed. The CNN input is the raw image sequence and baseline tabular features; the model learns feature hierarchies from data.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

**CNN model:** A 3D (or 2.5D) convolutional neural network with ResNet-18 or DenseNet-121 backbone, pre-trained on ImageNet and fine-tuned end-to-end. A regression head outputs a continuous risk score (0–100). Dropout (p=0.3) and batch normalization are applied throughout. Site and equipment are incorporated as a learned embedding or via site-conditional batch normalization to capture protocol variation.

**Baseline model:** Multivariate linear regression using only the same tabular features (demographics, prior clinical assessments, site), without access to the image. This represents current clinical practice and isolates the incremental value of image analysis. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: elastic net regularization hyperparameters (α, L1 ratio) are optimized via grid search on the validation set. Both models are evaluated on identical train/val/test splits and the same metrics.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for the CNN (learning rate, batch size, weight decay) are tuned using Bayesian optimization on the validation set, optimizing for validation MAE. Early stopping (patience=10 epochs) monitors validation MAE to prevent overfitting. **Critically: the test set is not inspected during hyperparameter tuning or model selection.** Once hyperparameters are locked, the model is retrained on the combined train+validation data (using the frozen hyperparameters) and evaluated once on the held-out test set.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** on the test set, reported in risk score units (0–100). MAE directly measures average prediction error in clinically interpretable terms. It is robust to outliers and does not require threshold tuning, making it appropriate for the regression setting and stakeholder focus on accurate risk stratification.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Concordance correlation coefficient (CCC) — assesses both correlation and calibration; ensures predictions reflect true risk magnitude, not just relative rankings.
- Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) — penalizes large outlier errors, reflecting clinical concern about serious mispredictions in high-risk patients.
- Stratified MAE by baseline risk quintile — detects any systematic performance degradation for very high or very low risk groups.
- Within-site MAE — reveals site-level biases and validates that imaging protocol variation does not drive performance differences.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

Imaging protocol variation across sites is the primary identified confound. We mitigate this through:
- **Stratified splitting:** Train/val/test sets are balanced by site, ensuring each site is represented proportionally in all folds.
- **Site-aware modeling:** Site and equipment model are included as features or learned embeddings in the CNN (e.g., via site-conditional batch normalization), allowing the model to learn site-specific imaging characteristics rather than treating them as noise.
- **Post-hoc stratified analysis:** We report MAE and CCC stratified by site to detect any hidden site-dependent performance collapse.
- **Sensitivity analysis:** A secondary model trained on a subset of data (excluding the largest or most atypical site) tests robustness to site-specific variation.

### 8. Scope of Claims

**What this experiment establishes:** Whether a CNN that processes echocardiogram images predicts 12-month heart disease risk more accurately (lower MAE, higher CCC) than linear regression on tabular clinical features alone, in a cohort of 10,000 diverse patients followed for 5 years. The experiment controls for imaging protocol variation through stratification and site-aware modeling.

**What it does not establish:** Generalization to entirely different imaging equipment or clinical settings outside the hospital network studied; causality or mechanistic understanding of why the CNN outperforms; clinical utility in real-world deployment (which would require prospective validation, cost-benefit analysis, and integration with clinical workflows).

must_not_claim_details.claim: The baseline model should have access to imaging data or a more complex architecture like a random forest to be a fair comparison.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The baseline is intentionally designed as multivariate linear regression on tabular features only to reflect current clinical practice, where risk assessment is based on structured patient data without image interpretation. Giving it imaging access or using a more complex model would deviate from the realistic comparison the experiment is meant to evaluate. The design explicitly ensures equivalent tuning effort, making the comparison valid and clinically meaningful.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The temporal split renders the validation set non-representative because it comes entirely after the training period, possibly introducing unaccounted-for distribution shifts.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Temporal splits are appropriate and in fact necessary in clinical longitudinal studies to prevent label leakage and simulate real-world deployment where models predict future outcomes. The stratification by baseline risk and site ensures representativeness across time periods, preserving key outcome distributions and controlling for known confounds. This design reflects best practice in prospective model validation.

hypothesis: An ensemble model combining XGBoost and LSTM on time-series transaction data predicts consumer spending in the retail sector more accurately than a traditional ARIMA model.
domain: Retail sector consumer spending prediction based on transaction data
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Ensemble vs. ARIMA for Retail Revenue Forecasting

## 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 60 months of historical transaction data from a single retail chain. Individual transactions (1M+) are aggregated into monthly revenue figures, which become the target variable for forecasting. Before analysis, data quality is verified: check for missing months, reconcile transaction sums against reported revenue records, and flag outliers attributable to system errors or data entry issues. The final dataset comprises 60 monthly revenue observations spanning years 1–5.

## 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Since this is a time series forecasting task, the data is split chronologically without shuffling. Months 1–36 form the training set, months 37–48 form the validation set, and months 49–60 form the test set. This 60%–20%–20% split is chosen to reflect how the model will be deployed: trained on historical data and evaluated on unseen future periods. Temporal ordering prevents look-ahead bias and ensures that validation and test represent a realistic forecasting scenario. Any shuffling would break the temporal structure and inflate reported accuracy.

## 3. Feature Engineering

Features are constructed from the training set and applied consistently to validation and test sets:

- **Lagged revenue**: t-1, t-3, t-12 (short-term trend and annual seasonality)
- **Category composition**: proportion of monthly revenue from top 5 item categories (fit on training data distribution)
- **Temporal indicators**: month-of-year (one-hot encoded) and a binary flag for holiday months (defined a priori)
- **Detrended revenue**: linear trend fitted on months 1–36, subtracted from all monthly values

All preprocessing (scaling, trend removal, category definition) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied to validation and test to prevent data leakage.

## 4. Models and Baseline

The ensemble combines two sub-models:
- **XGBoost**: trained on tabular features (lags, category mix, temporal indicators)
- **LSTM**: trained on sequences of 12 prior months, predicting the next month

The ensemble output is the equal-weight average of both predictions. The baseline is an ARIMA(p,d,q) model fit on training data using auto-selection (grid search over p,d,q ∈ {0,1,2} × {0,1} × {0,1,2}, selecting the lowest AIC). The ARIMA model uses only univariate revenue; no external regressors are added. Both ensemble and baseline receive equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated identically.

## 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for XGBoost (learning rate, max_depth, n_estimators) and LSTM (hidden units, dropout) are tuned via grid search on the validation set (months 37–48), optimizing for validation MAPE. The final model configuration is locked before test evaluation. The test set is held completely untouched during all tuning and selection phases.

## 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) on the test set. MAPE directly aligns with the stakeholder objective of accurate monthly revenue forecasting for inventory and promotional planning. It is scale-independent and interpretable as a percentage error across different revenue levels.

**Secondary metrics**:
- MAE (dollars) — absolute forecast error
- RMSE — penalizes large errors
- Directional accuracy — percentage of months where forecast direction matches actual
- Ljung-Box test on residuals — checks for systematic unpredicted patterns

## 7. Confounds and Scope

External market events (economic downturns, pandemics) may affect consumer spending patterns. These confounds do not invalidate the comparison if both models are evaluated on the same data; however, they constrain the claim. The experiment establishes whether the ensemble outperforms ARIMA *on this historical dataset under observed conditions*. To assess robustness, sensitivity analysis is performed: test performance is stratified by whether months fall within documented event periods or normal periods. Major events in the test window are logged and reported separately.

## 8. Test Set Evaluation

Once model selection is complete, both the fitted ensemble and baseline ARIMA model generate predictions for months 49–60 (test set). MAPE and secondary metrics are computed. No retraining or parameter adjustment occurs after test evaluation. The test metric comparison directly answers whether the ensemble hypothesis holds on this data.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The temporal split (60% train, 20% val, 20% test) is unbalanced or too small for reliable model validation.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: For a time series with 60 monthly observations, a 36-12-12 split is standard and methodologically sound. It preserves temporal integrity and provides sufficient data for training and evaluation, especially given annual seasonality patterns. The split mirrors real-world deployment where models are trained on several years of history and evaluated on future months.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using MAPE as the primary metric is problematic due to potential division-by-zero or extreme sensitivity when actual values are near zero.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The retail revenue data does not trend toward zero—monthly revenues are consistently high. MAPE is explicitly justified by stakeholder needs for percentage-level accuracy in inventory and promotion planning. It remains interpretable and appropriate in this operational context.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The ensemble uses equal weighting without optimization, which may suboptimal.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The hypothesis compares an ensemble approach to ARIMA, not an optimally weighted ensemble. Equal weighting is a legitimate and interpretable choice that avoids introducing additional complexity or overfitting during validation. It fulfills the goal of testing whether combining models helps, without requiring optimal fusion.

sound_design_reference:
# Experiment Design: Ensemble vs. ARIMA for Retail Revenue Forecasting

## 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 60 months of historical transaction data from a single retail chain. Individual transactions (1M+) are aggregated into monthly revenue figures, which become the target variable for forecasting. Before analysis, data quality is verified: check for missing months, reconcile transaction sums against reported revenue records, and flag outliers attributable to system errors or data entry issues. The final dataset comprises 60 monthly revenue observations spanning years 1–5.

## 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Since this is a time series forecasting task, the data is split chronologically without shuffling. Months 1–36 form the training set, months 37–48 form the validation set, and months 49–60 form the test set. This 60%–20%–20% split is chosen to reflect how the model will be deployed: trained on historical data and evaluated on unseen future periods. Temporal ordering prevents look-ahead bias and ensures that validation and test represent a realistic forecasting scenario. Any shuffling would break the temporal structure and inflate reported accuracy.

## 3. Feature Engineering

Features are constructed from the training set and applied consistently to validation and test sets:

- **Lagged revenue**: t-1, t-3, t-12 (short-term trend and annual seasonality)
- **Category composition**: proportion of monthly revenue from top 5 item categories (fit on training data distribution)
- **Temporal indicators**: month-of-year (one-hot encoded) and a binary flag for holiday months (defined a priori)
- **Detrended revenue**: linear trend fitted on months 1–36, subtracted from all monthly values

All preprocessing (scaling, trend removal, category definition) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied to validation and test to prevent data leakage.

## 4. Models and Baseline

The ensemble combines two sub-models:
- **XGBoost**: trained on tabular features (lags, category mix, temporal indicators)
- **LSTM**: trained on sequences of 12 prior months, predicting the next month

The ensemble output is the equal-weight average of both predictions. The baseline is an ARIMA(p,d,q) model fit on training data using auto-selection (grid search over p,d,q ∈ {0,1,2} × {0,1} × {0,1,2}, selecting the lowest AIC). The ARIMA model uses only univariate revenue; no external regressors are added. Both ensemble and baseline receive equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated identically.

## 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for XGBoost (learning rate, max_depth, n_estimators) and LSTM (hidden units, dropout) are tuned via grid search on the validation set (months 37–48), optimizing for validation MAPE. The final model configuration is locked before test evaluation. The test set is held completely untouched during all tuning and selection phases.

## 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) on the test set. MAPE directly aligns with the stakeholder objective of accurate monthly revenue forecasting for inventory and promotional planning. It is scale-independent and interpretable as a percentage error across different revenue levels.

**Secondary metrics**:
- MAE (dollars) — absolute forecast error
- RMSE — penalizes large errors
- Directional accuracy — percentage of months where forecast direction matches actual
- Ljung-Box test on residuals — checks for systematic unpredicted patterns

## 7. Confounds and Scope

External market events (economic downturns, pandemics) may affect consumer spending patterns. These confounds do not invalidate the comparison if both models are evaluated on the same data; however, they constrain the claim. The experiment establishes whether the ensemble outperforms ARIMA *on this historical dataset under observed conditions*. To assess robustness, sensitivity analysis is performed: test performance is stratified by whether months fall within documented event periods or normal periods. Major events in the test window are logged and reported separately.

## 8. Test Set Evaluation

Once model selection is complete, both the fitted ensemble and baseline ARIMA model generate predictions for months 49–60 (test set). MAPE and secondary metrics are computed. No retraining or parameter adjustment occurs after test evaluation. The test metric comparison directly answers whether the ensemble hypothesis holds on this data.

hypothesis: Utilizing a multi-modal model that combines genomic data and clinical notes predicts patient response to immunotherapy better than using genomic data alone in melanoma patients.
domain: Oncology / precision medicine for melanoma treatment
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Modal Prediction of Immunotherapy Response in Melanoma

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

We will use a retrospective cohort of 500 melanoma patients from a single institution or multi-site consortium with standardized treatment protocols, spanning 5 years of clinical records. The data includes three modalities:

- **Genomic data**: Tumor mutational burden (TMB), driver mutations (BRAF, NRAS, NF1, KIT), MSI/dMMR status, HLA predictions, and mutational signatures derived from sequencing panels.
- **Clinical tabular data**: ECOG performance status, LDH level, number of metastatic sites, TNM stage, and prior therapy exposure.
- **Clinical notes**: Pathology reports and treatment summaries, processed via a pretrained BioBERT encoder to generate fixed-length feature vectors.

The outcome label is treatment response, classified per RECIST 1.1 at 12 weeks post-treatment initiation and confirmed by radiologist review. For primary analysis, response is binarized into responders (CR+PR) versus non-responders (SD+PD). All labels are assigned prospectively at the point of clinical care and frozen before model development begins.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

We employ a **stratified random split** to preserve the distribution of response class and key demographic strata (age, stage, ECOG) across train, validation, and test sets. Stratification is justified because it ensures each fold represents the patient population the model will encounter in deployment and avoids selection bias. We do not use a temporal split because treatment response is a fixed post-hoc outcome; temporal ordering reflects documentation sequence, not predictive information flow.

**Split allocation**: 60% training (n≈300), 20% validation (n≈100), 20% held-out test (n≈100). A fixed random seed is documented to ensure reproducibility.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing steps are **fit on the training set only** and applied identically to validation and test:

- **Genomic features**: Z-score normalization and batch correction (if multi-site data) fit on train genomic data.
- **Tabular clinical features**: Missing values imputed using the training set median; missingness indicators included as binary features.
- **Clinical notes**: Tokenized and encoded using a pretrained BioBERT model (not retrained on this cohort to avoid label leakage). The encoder produces a 768-dimensional vector per patient.

All features are generated before model training; no adaptive feature selection or dimensionality reduction is performed on validation or test data.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **multi-modal model** is a neural network with three branches:

- Genomic branch: 2–3 fully connected layers (64–128 units, ReLU, batch norm, 0.3 dropout).
- Clinical tabular branch: 2 fully connected layers (32–64 units, ReLU, batch norm).
- Clinical text branch: BioBERT embeddings passed through 1 FC layer (64 units, ReLU).
- Fusion: Concatenated outputs through 2 FC layers (128 units, ReLU, 0.3 dropout), then sigmoid output for binary classification.

The **baseline model** is identical in architecture but uses genomic features only, excluding clinical tabular and text branches. This isolates the predictive contribution of clinical data while holding architectural capacity and tuning effort constant. Both models are optimized with Adam and the same learning rate schedule; hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set with an identical search space and compute budget.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). AUROC measures discrimination ability across all classification thresholds and is robust to class imbalance, making it appropriate for the oncology team's goal of identifying responders to allocate resources and tailor therapy. It does not require upfront threshold specification and allows clinicians to choose an operating point based on risk tolerance.

**Secondary metrics**:
- Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), sensitive to responder detection in the minority class.
- Sensitivity at 90% specificity, to minimize false positives in a resource-constrained setting.
- Expected Calibration Error (ECE), to ensure predicted probabilities reflect true treatment response likelihood.
- Cohen's kappa on binarized and 4-class response labels.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

The validation set (n≈100) is used to select final hyperparameters via grid search or Bayesian optimization. AUROC on the validation set drives model selection and early stopping. The test set is not consulted during development and is held out completely until final model freezing.

### 7. Test Evaluation and Reporting

Once the multi-modal and baseline models are finalized, a single evaluation pass is performed on the held-out test set. Test metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via stratified bootstrap (1000 replicates). **Subgroup analyses** stratify performance by stage, ECOG, TMB quartile, and BRAF status to assess whether multi-modal gains are uniform or concentrated in specific patient subgroups.

### 8. Confound Management

Stratification ensures demographic balance across folds. Demographic and prognostic variables (age, stage, ECOG, prior therapy, LDH) are included as model features, allowing the model to learn their associations with response. Missing data mechanisms are assessed; if missingness is informative (e.g., sicker patients have fewer notes), missingness indicators are included and reported. This design predicts treatment response; it does not establish causality.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The stratified random split is inappropriate because the data spans 5 years; a temporal split should have been used to simulate real-world deployment over time.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Temporal splits are appropriate when modeling predictive performance over time (e.g., concept drift). Here, the hypothesis concerns the static predictive power of clinical notes beyond genomics — not time-based generalization. Stratified random splitting ensures each fold represents the target population distribution and avoids selection bias. Retaining temporal ordering would not provide additional validity and could harm statistical efficiency without evidence of temporal confounding.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using AUROC as the primary metric is suboptimal because the oncology team cares more about catching all responders, so sensitivity or F1-score should be prioritized.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: AUROC is the correct choice here because it evaluates model discrimination across all thresholds without committing to a single operating point. The clinical goal is to support threshold selection based on risk tolerance (e.g., high sensitivity vs. high specificity). AUROC enables post-hoc decision-making. Secondary metrics (AUPRC, sensitivity at 90% specificity) directly address responder detection performance if needed.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The baseline model should be a simple logistic regression on genomic data, not a neural network with the same architecture.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Using a neural network baseline with identical architecture isolates the contribution of multi-modal data, not differences in modeling capacity. A logistic regression conflate architectural limitations with data value. This design uses fair comparison principles: same model class, tuning, and initialization — ensuring any improvement is attributable to additional modalities, not model superiority.

hypothesis: Using a transformer-based model fine-tuned on patient-reported outcomes predicts depression severity scores from electronic health records better than traditional regression models using demographic and clinical features.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML (EHR data and patient-reported outcomes)
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Depression Severity Prediction from EHR Data

### 1. Research Question and Scope

This experiment tests whether a transformer-based model fine-tuned on clinical notes and structured EHR data predicts depression severity scores with lower prediction error (higher accuracy) than traditional regression using only demographics and clinical features. Success is measured as statistically significant reduction in Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on held-out test data. The experiment does not establish causation or mechanism; it measures predictive performance on a prospective-in-time hold-out set.

### 2. Data Strategy

The dataset comprises 5 years of EHR records from a single health system (approximately 10,000 patients). The label is the most recently recorded patient-reported depression severity score (e.g., PHQ-9, range 0–27) within 30 days of a clinical encounter, ensuring temporal alignment between clinical observations and the outcome measure. All data is extracted once; no real-time streaming or incremental updates are performed.

**Data quality handling:** Records with missing depression severity scores are excluded. Patients with fewer than one recorded outcome measure are excluded. Clinical notes with missing or truncated text are flagged; truncated notes are excluded from the analysis. Missing values in structured features (demographics, medication, comorbidities) are imputed using training set statistics (median for continuous, mode for categorical).

**Distribution alignment:** The local health system's patient population and data completeness over the 5-year period are documented. The hypothesis assumes that future deployment will occur within the same system or a similar population; significant distribution shifts (e.g., major changes in patient demographics or EHR documentation practices over the 5 years) are identified and reported.

### 3. Split Strategy and Temporal Design

Data is split temporally to prevent information leakage and to simulate prospective prediction:

- **Training:** Months 1–36 (first 3 years)
- **Validation:** Months 37–48 (year 4)
- **Test:** Months 49–60 (year 5)

All data at the patient level (i.e., all records for a single patient) are assigned to a single split; no patient appears in multiple splits. This prevents data leakage from longitudinal patient trajectories.

Temporal splitting is critical for this hypothesis because (1) depression severity evolves over time and future clinical information is unavailable at prediction time, (2) the model must generalize to unseen future time periods, not merely interpolate, and (3) confounds such as seasonal trends or evolving clinical practice are partially addressed by training on an early period and testing on a later one.

### 4. Feature Engineering

**Transformer model input:**
- Clinical notes (all encounter notes in the 12 months prior to the prediction date) are tokenized using a biomedical tokenizer (BioBERT or Clinical BERT) and embedded using the pre-trained transformer.
- Structured features (age, sex, race/ethnicity, recent diagnoses as one-hot encoded indicators, active medications by class, visit frequency, days since last visit) are separately embedded and concatenated with note embeddings.
- All continuous features are standardized using training set mean and standard deviation. Categorical features are one-hot encoded using training set vocabulary.

**Baseline model input:**
- Demographics: age, sex, race/ethnicity.
- Clinical features: count of depression-related diagnoses in the prior 12 months, current use of antidepressant medication (yes/no), Charlson comorbidity index.
- Visit cadence: days since last encounter, total visits in the prior 12 months.
- No clinical notes are used in the baseline.
- Features are standardized and encoded identically to the transformer model.

**Preprocessing:** All scalers, encoders, and imputers are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–36) and applied without refitting to validation and test data. This prevents leakage.

No feature is constructed that encodes the label or uses information from after the prediction date.

### 5. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:** A pre-trained biomedical transformer (e.g., Clinical BERT) is fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set. A linear regression head outputs a single continuous depression severity score. The model is trained using mean squared error (MSE) loss with standard backpropagation. Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, number of fine-tuning epochs, dropout rate, weight decay) are selected via grid search on the validation set (see Section 6).

**Baseline model:** Ridge regression (linear regression with L2 penalty) trained on the traditional feature set. The ridge penalty α is selected via grid search on the validation set. Ridge regression is chosen to match the complexity-control effort applied to the transformer.

**Fairness of comparison:** Both models receive the same train/val/test splits, preprocessing, and hyperparameter tuning effort. The transformer's advantage is access to unstructured clinical notes; the baseline uses only structured features. A fair conclusion is that if the transformer achieves lower validation RMSE, the improvement comes from either (1) richer information in notes or (2) better feature learning via the transformer architecture. A secondary ablation (transformer without notes) isolates architectural effects.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 37–48):
- **Transformer:** Grid search over learning rates {1e-5, 5e-5, 1e-4}, batch sizes {16, 32}, and dropout rates {0.1, 0.3, 0.5}. The configuration minimizing validation RMSE is selected.
- **Baseline:** Grid search over ridge penalty α ∈ {10^-3, 10^-2, ..., 10^3}. The α minimizing validation RMSE is selected.

The test set is not touched during this process. After model selection, both models are evaluated exactly once on the held-out test set (months 49–60).

### 7. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) on the test set. RMSE is chosen because large prediction errors (e.g., missing a high-risk patient or incorrectly flagging a low-risk patient) are penalized more heavily, aligning with clinical risk stratification. RMSE is also interpretable in PHQ-9 units (0–27 scale).

**Secondary metrics:**
- Pearson and Spearman correlation (monotonic agreement between predicted and observed scores).
- Mean Absolute Error (average magnitude of prediction error).
- R² (proportion of variance explained).
- Calibration curve (predicted mean score vs. observed mean score by decile of predicted risk).
- **Stratified RMSE by social determinants** (race/ethnicity, insurance type) to detect bias.

### 8. Controls for Confounds

Social determinants of health (race/ethnicity, insurance type, neighborhood SES) are included as features in both models, allowing them to adjust for these confounds. Stratified test set evaluation reveals whether the model's predictions are systematically biased across demographic groups. A sensitivity analysis retrains the transformer without clinical notes; if performance degrades minimally, the improvement may be driven by confound adjustment rather than note information. Temporal confounds (seasonal trends, evolving clinical documentation) are partially addressed by the temporal split itself.

### 9. Test Set and Reporting

The test set is held completely separate and used only for final evaluation. All reported metrics (RMSE, correlation, calibration, stratified performance) are computed on the test set. Confidence intervals (95%) are computed via bootstrap resampling of the test set (with replacement, stratified by patient) to quantify uncertainty. The experiment will clearly state which population and time period the test results generalize to.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The temporal split appears unbalanced, with more recent years used for test than training, which could reduce training data and increase variance.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Temporal splits should reflect prospective deployment; assigning the most recent data to test is methodologically correct here. The training period (3 years) is sufficiently long for stable model learning given the cohort size (~10,000 patients), and imbalance in time-based splits is expected and valid in time-series settings.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using RMSE as the primary metric may be inappropriate because it penalizes large errors more heavily, possibly overemphasizing outliers in depression severity prediction.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: RMSE is explicitly justified by clinical context: mispredictions of high-risk patients (outliers) are the most consequential for care decisions. Furthermore, depression severity is a continuous outcome, and RMSE is both standard and interpretable in PHQ-9 units, supporting stakeholder needs for accurate risk stratification.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The baseline model uses Ridge regression instead of ordinary least squares (OLS), which introduces unnecessary regularization and may disadvantage the baseline.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Using Ridge regression with tuned regularization is a sound choice to ensure a fair comparison: both models undergo similar complexity control (hyperparameter tuning). OLS without penalty could overfit, making a comparison with a regularized transformer model unfair. The tuned baseline matches best practices for small-to-medium EHR datasets.

hypothesis: A recurrent neural network (RNN) model trained on sequential patient visit data predicts 30-day hospital readmission risk better than a traditional logistic regression model using demographic and clinical features.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML, specifically hospital readmissions
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: RNN vs. Logistic Regression for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 24 months of patient visit records from a single metropolitan hospital, covering approximately 10,000 patient admissions with readmission labels. Data is extracted from the electronic health record (EHR) system and includes demographics (age, sex, insurance type), diagnoses (ICD-10 codes), procedures (CPT codes), visit timestamps, and the binary outcome: readmission within 30 days of discharge.

To ensure label quality, we audit 200 randomly sampled records through manual chart review, checking for misdating, miscoding, or missing readmissions. Any systematic errors discovered are corrected; random labeling noise is accepted as inherent to the data source.

**Data distribution matching:** We acknowledge that data is from a single hospital and may not represent other institutions. This is a limitation noted in scope, but within this site, the data reflects the target deployment population.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

We divide data chronologically: **training (months 1–12), validation (months 13–18), test (months 19–24).** This temporal split is essential for three reasons:

- **Causality:** The hypothesis concerns prediction of future readmission; a temporal split ensures no future information leaks into training.
- **Deployment alignment:** In practice, the model will be trained on historical data and applied prospectively. The split mirrors this workflow.
- **Patient integrity:** Each patient's full visit sequence is assigned to exactly one set based on their index admission date, preventing data leakage across sets.

We check for readmission prevalence drift across time periods. If prevalence shifts significantly (e.g., due to changes in hospital discharge practices), we report this as a confound that could explain performance differences.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**RNN input:** Visit sequences are represented as timestamped events. Each visit is encoded as a vector containing: (a) days elapsed since the prior visit, (b) primary diagnosis (one-hot encoded from top 50 diagnoses, with a catch-all 'other' category), (c) binary indicators for top 20 procedures, (d) visit type (emergency, inpatient, outpatient), and (e) total number of diagnoses at that visit. Sequences are variable-length and padded/truncated to the 95th percentile observed length. Demographic features (age, sex, insurance) are concatenated to the final hidden state prior to the output layer.

**Baseline (logistic regression) input:** Fixed features include age, sex, insurance type, count of prior visits in the past 12 months, count of distinct diagnoses in the past 12 months, binary indicators for high-risk diagnoses (CHF, COPD, diabetes), and count of emergency visits in the past 12 months. This represents a standard clinical approach and is intentionally simpler than the RNN's sequential input; this difference in feature richness is expected and part of the hypothesis.

**Preprocessing:** All transformations are fit on the training set only and applied uniformly to validation and test. Numeric features are standardized using training set statistics (z-score). Categorical features are one-hot encoded using the training vocabulary. Missing values in numeric features are imputed with the training set mean; missing categorical values receive a dedicated 'missing' category. This prevents data leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**RNN model:** A two-layer LSTM with 64 hidden units per layer, 0.3 dropout, trained for up to 100 epochs with early stopping (patience=10) on validation loss. The model outputs a single sigmoid-activated probability.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression (L2 regularization) trained on identical data, with hyperparameter tuning via grid search on the validation set using the primary metric (AUROC; see below). This ensures equivalent tuning effort and a fair comparison.

**Why this baseline is appropriate:** Logistic regression is the traditional approach in clinical prediction and represents the standard of care. It uses simpler, manually engineered features. If the RNN outperforms logistic regression, it suggests that sequential modeling or neural capacity provides an advantage; if not, the simpler baseline suffices.

### 5. Handling Class Imbalance

Readmission prevalence is expected to be 15–20%. We apply class weights during training (weight_0 = 1, weight_1 = n_neg / n_pos) in the RNN's loss function. We do not oversample or undersample, as these would distort the temporal structure of sequences. We report all metrics stratified by patient demographics (age, insurance, prior readmission history) to detect any bias introduced by weighting.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUROC (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve).** This metric is chosen because:
- The model is used to rank patients by readmission risk, and AUROC captures performance across all possible decision thresholds.
- It is unaffected by class imbalance and does not require threshold selection.
- It aligns with hospital operations: identifying high-risk patients enables stratified intervention, and AUROC measures how well the model separates risk strata.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision-Recall AUC (to emphasize minority class performance)
- Sensitivity and specificity at the threshold that maximizes F1 on validation data (operational utility)
- Expected Calibration Error in deciles (do predicted probabilities reflect true readmission rates?)
- Subgroup performance: AUROC separately for age (<65 vs ≥65), insurance type, and prior readmission history.

### 7. Model Selection and Test Set Procedure

Both the RNN and baseline undergo hyperparameter tuning on the validation set. The model achieving the highest AUROC on validation is retained. The test set is held until final evaluation and is used only once. We report AUROC, secondary metrics, and subgroup analyses on the test set without modification.

### 8. Confound Controls

**Temporal confounds:** Hospital practices (discharge criteria, follow-up protocols) may change over 24 months. We test for trend in readmission prevalence and report any significant drift.

**Demographic confounds:** Patient age, insurance, and history differ across cohorts and independently influence readmission risk. We stratify all analyses by these factors. If the RNN advantage exists only in one subgroup, generalizability is limited.

**Data quality:** We separately compute metrics on the 200 manually audited records as a high-confidence sensitivity analysis.

**Mechanism of improvement:** To test whether the RNN's advantage derives from sequencing or simply model capacity, we train a non-sequential neural baseline (MLP) on concatenated visit features. If both neural models outperform logistic regression similarly, the gain may be due to capacity, not sequencing.

### 9. Scope and Limitations

This experiment establishes whether an RNN outperforms logistic regression at this hospital during the 24-month period. It does **not** establish external generalizability, causality, or clinical utility when predictions are acted upon. Results are reported alongside these caveats.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The temporal split (months 1–12 for training, 13–18 for validation, 19–24 for test) may not have enough data in each set, especially the validation set, potentially leading to unstable hyperparameter tuning.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The split is justified by the need for temporal consistency and causal validity in a predictive setting. With approximately 10,000 admissions over 24 months (~400–500 per month), each split has sufficient data (4,000–6,000 admissions) to support training, hyperparameter tuning, and final evaluation. The design intentionally mirrors real-world deployment and avoids leakage, making smaller but temporally clean splits preferable over larger shuffled splits.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using class weights instead of oversampling for imbalance is inappropriate because it may bias the model or distort risk estimates.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Class weighting is a well-established and appropriate method for handling class imbalance in temporal sequence modeling, where oversampling would create synthetic sequences and break temporal dependencies. The design correctly avoids distorting the temporal dynamics, and the use of class weights is transparently reported and paired with stratified metric reporting to detect bias.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The primary metric AUROC is unsuitable for imbalanced medical prediction tasks; Precision-Recall AUC should be used instead.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: While Precision-Recall AUC is valuable under imbalance, AUROC is appropriate here because the hospital’s operational goal is risk stratification and ranking patients by risk across varying intervention thresholds. AUROC evaluates the model’s ability to separate classes across all thresholds, which aligns with clinical prioritization workflows. The design wisely includes Precision-Recall AUC as a secondary metric to provide a complementary view.

task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hybrid CNN-LSTM vs. Logistic Regression for Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort Definition

The analysis uses structured EHR data from 10,000 chronic disease patients spanning 5 years. Each observation represents a single index discharge event with a 30-day follow-up window and a 180-day look-back period for feature construction. The binary readmission label is defined as any unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge (standard clinical definition). Elective and scheduled procedures are excluded from the readmission definition.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To respect causal ordering and prevent information leakage, the data is divided by discharge date into three sequential windows: (1) Training: discharges from years 1–3 (~6,000 patients); (2) Validation: discharges from year 4, first half (~1,500 patients); (3) Test: discharges from year 4, second half and year 5 (~2,500 patients). Within each window, the cohort is stratified by readmission class (0/1) to maintain class balance representation. This temporal split reflects deployment reality—a model trained on historical discharges is applied to prospective cohorts. The stratified component ensures that class imbalance (anticipated at 15–25% readmission rate) does not bias any split.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are constructed from the 180-day look-back window prior to discharge and encode four dimensions: (1) Demographics and baseline risk (age, comorbidity index); (2) Temporal utilization patterns (prior admission counts in rolling 30/60/90-day windows, time since last admission, admission frequency stability); (3) Index admission characteristics (length of stay, admission source, primary diagnosis); (4) Medication and clinical instability (medication burden, recent abnormal labs, vital sign variability). Temporal features explicitly capture sequence structure and irregular visit intervals, enabling the LSTM to learn patterns of clinical deterioration.

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only: mean imputation for missing values, z-score standardization for continuous features, and one-hot encoding for categorical variables (fitted on training vocabulary). These transformations are applied identically to validation and test sets, preventing leakage and ensuring honest out-of-sample performance estimates.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The hybrid CNN-LSTM model processes temporal sequences: a 1D CNN (32–64 filters) extracts local patterns in visit dynamics, followed by LSTM layers (128 units) that capture long-range dependencies. The output passes through fully connected layers (64 units, ReLU) to a sigmoid output. Dropout (0.3) and L2 regularization (0.001) prevent overfitting.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on the identical feature set and preprocessed using the same fit-on-train approach. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning on the validation set (learning rate, regularization strength, class weights). This ensures the comparison isolates the value of the temporal architecture, not differential tuning effort.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set using AUROC as the selection criterion. The optimal models (CNN-LSTM and logistic regression) are then frozen and evaluated once on the test set. No model selection, threshold tuning, or metric inspection occurs on the test set.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC). This metric reflects the operational goal—correctly ranking patients by readmission risk across the full spectrum to guide stratified intervention. AUROC is robust to class imbalance and avoids arbitrary threshold selection.

**Secondary metrics:** Precision-Recall AUC (more sensitive to class imbalance than AUROC), sensitivity at 90% specificity (ensures clinically safe false negative rates), and calibration (Expected Calibration Error—essential because readmission scores are communicated to clinicians). Subgroup analysis by age, comorbidity level, and admission source assesses generalization and detects disparities.

### 7. Addressing Confounding

Variable follow-up care intensity is addressed through feature inclusion (outpatient visit frequency, pharmacy refill patterns, care coordination flags) and subgroup stratification. The model learns associations between follow-up intensity and readmission but makes predictions conditional on observed care patterns—not counterfactual predictions. Subgroup performance is tracked; substantial degradation in low-follow-up groups signals uncontrolled confounding. Results are scoped as predictive accuracy given current care practices, not causal estimates of readmission determinants.

### 8. Success Criteria

The hypothesis is supported if the CNN-LSTM achieves meaningfully higher validation and test AUROC than logistic regression (e.g., ≥0.03 absolute improvement), with comparable or superior calibration and sensitivity, and consistent subgroup performance across follow-up intensity strata.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The temporal split stratifies by readmission status, which could distort real-world deployment performance by over-representing positive (readmission) cases.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Stratification within temporally separated splits preserves the temporal order while ensuring sufficient positive cases in each split for stable evaluation—this is appropriate for rare-event prediction and does not introduce leakage or bias under the hypothesis. The actual temporal distribution is reflected in the overall cohort, and stratification only affects sampling within time windows to handle class imbalance.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using AUROC as the primary metric is inappropriate due to class imbalance in readmission data.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: AUROC is robust to class imbalance and directly aligns with the operational use case—ranking patients by risk for stratified intervention. In clinical deployment, clinicians prioritize identifying high-risk patients across the full risk spectrum, not optimizing at a single threshold, making AUROC a valid and widely accepted metric in this domain.

hypothesis: A neural network model that utilizes multimodal data (including EHR notes and patient demographics) outperforms a traditional logistic regression model in predicting post-operative complications in patients undergoing orthopedic surgery.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for orthopedic surgery outcomes prediction
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Multimodal Neural Network vs. Logistic Regression for Post-Operative Complication Prediction

### 1. Data Selection and Preparation

We would use electronic health records from a single hospital system spanning 5 years (60 months). The dataset comprises all patients undergoing orthopedic surgery with complete structured data (age, sex, BMI, ASA grade, preoperative labs including hemoglobin, albumin, creatinine), unstructured clinical text (preoperative assessment notes, operative reports, intraoperative notes), and verified post-operative outcomes. We would extract all patients with documented complications (surgical site infection, VTE, pneumonia, acute kidney injury, readmission, or return to OR) within 90 days post-surgery and balance these against uncomplicated cases.

The label is defined a priori using institutional complication criteria and validated against ICD-10 codes, with clinical adjudication by a physician blinded to model predictions to ensure consistency.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split Strategy

We would employ a temporal split with stratification. Months 1–36 are designated for training; months 37–48 for validation; months 49–60 for held-out test evaluation. This temporal strategy prevents information leakage and ensures the model generalizes to genuinely future patients, reflecting real-world deployment where clinical practice, protocols, and patient populations evolve.

Within each temporal window, we would stratify by surgeon (or surgical team) and complication rate quartile to ensure that surgical practice variability and case complexity are balanced across splits. This stratification mitigates confounding from individual surgeon technique, care protocols, and case selection that would otherwise correlate with outcomes.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured features include demographics (age, sex, BMI, ASA score), preoperative laboratory values (hemoglobin, albumin, creatinine, comorbidity burden), surgery type (one-hot encoded), and surgeon identifier (fixed effect). Unstructured clinical notes (preoperative and intraoperative) are embedded using a pretrained clinical language model (e.g., SciBERT or BioBERT fine-tuned on MIMIC) to generate fixed-length contextual vector representations. Derived features include days from admission to surgery.

All preprocessing—scaling of labs, imputation of missing values, text vectorization, and dimensionality reduction—is fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test data. No statistics or learned parameters from validation or test sets inform preprocessing, eliminating leakage.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The neural network employs a multimodal architecture: a structured data branch (2–3 fully connected layers with batch normalization and dropout) processes demographics and labs; a text branch (frozen pretrained embeddings → pooling → fully connected layers) processes clinical notes. Branches are concatenated and passed through additional fully connected layers, culminating in a sigmoid output.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on the same structured feature set (demographics, labs, surgery type, surgeon effects) using identical preprocessing and equivalent tuning effort. Critically, the baseline does not incorporate text embeddings. This design isolates the hypothesis: the improvement comes from multimodal integration and neural capacity, not from additional features.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Both the neural network and logistic regression undergo hyperparameter optimization on the validation set (months 37–48). For the neural network, we tune layer sizes, dropout rates, learning rate, batch size, and optimization algorithm. For logistic regression, we optimize L1/L2 regularization strength. Model selection uses the primary metric (sensitivity at 85% fixed specificity) evaluated on validation data. The best model is selected and locked before test evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is sensitivity (recall) at a fixed specificity of 85%. This reflects clinical priorities: identifying at-risk patients for preventive intervention is the priority. A sensitivity floor ensures broad capture of complications while maintaining operational feasibility (clinician workload). Secondary metrics include AUROC, AUPRC (appropriate under potential class imbalance), calibration curves, and F1 score.

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Reporting

The test set (months 49–60) is evaluated exactly once, at the conclusion of the experiment. Final performance is reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap. Subgroup analysis stratifies test results by surgeon, complication severity, age, and surgery type to detect performance heterogeneity and identify generalization limitations.

### 8. Scope and Limitations

This design establishes whether the multimodal neural network achieves superior discrimination and sensitivity compared to logistic regression on structured data alone, within a single hospital system over a 5-year window. It does not establish whether the model is ready for clinical deployment (external validation on other hospitals, prospective validation, and cost-benefit analysis are required). Surgeon and practice-level confounding is mitigated through stratification and fixed effects modeling, but residual unmeasured confounding from individual surgeon skill cannot be eliminated.

must_not_claim_details.claim: The baseline model is unfair because it does not include text features.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The exclusion of text features in the baseline logistic regression is intentional and methodologically sound; it isolates the effect of multimodal neural architecture rather than additional data sources, ensuring a fair comparison focused on architectural superiority.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Temporal split might cause data imbalance across splits due to evolving surgical practices.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is appropriate and necessary here to simulate real-world deployment; imbalance over time is expected and accounts for clinical drift, which the design explicitly aims to evaluate.

must_not_claim_details.claim: Using sensitivity at 85% specificity prioritizes recall over precision and may lead to too many false positives.
must_not_claim_details.why_wrong: The choice of sensitivity at fixed specificity is clinically justified: identifying at-risk patients is the primary goal, and a high-specificity threshold ensures that alerts are manageable and operationally feasible.
